stem: Let \( F(x) = \int_{[[c]]}^{x} \left( t^{2} + ([[b]])t + ([[cc]]) \right) dt \). Which of the following is a critical number of \( F \), i.e. a value of \( x \) at which \( F'(x) = 0 \)?
solution: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, \( F'(x) = x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[cc]]) \). Setting \( F'(x) = 0 \) and factoring gives \( (x - ([[r1]]))(x - ([[r2]])) = 0 \), so the critical numbers are \( x = [[r1]] \) and \( x = [[r2]] \); \( [[answer]] \) is one of them.
distractor_rationales: This is the sum of the two critical numbers, not a critical number itself.
This is the product of the two critical numbers (the constant term), not a root.
This reads off the linear coefficient of \( F'(x) \) as if it were a root.
This sign-flips one of the roots.
This sign-flips the other root.
This is not a root of \( F'(x) \).
This is not a root of \( F'(x) \).

stem: Let \( F(x) = \int_{[[c]]}^{x} (t - ([[r1]]))(t - ([[r2]]))\, dt \). The integrand \( f(t) = (t-([[r1]]))(t-([[r2]])) \) is negative for \( t \) strictly between its two roots and positive outside. At which critical number does \( F \) have a LOCAL MINIMUM?
solution: Since \( f(t) \) is negative on \( ([[r1]], [[r2]]) \) and positive for \( t > [[r2]] \), \( F'=f \) changes from negative to positive at \( x = [[r2]] \); that sign change (decreasing to increasing) marks a local minimum of \( F \). At \( x=[[r1]] \), \( f \) changes from positive to negative, giving a local maximum instead. So the local minimum is at \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the OTHER critical number, where \( F \) has a local maximum, not a minimum.
This is the sum of the two critical numbers, not a critical number itself.
This is the midpoint of the two roots, which is not generally a critical number of \( F \).
This is the product of the two roots (the constant term of \( f \)), not a critical number.
This is off by 3 from the correct value.
This is off by 3 from the correct value.
This sign-flips the correct root.

stem: Let \( F(x) = \int_{[[c]]}^{x} (t-([[r1]]))(t-([[r2]]))(t-([[r3]]))\, dt \). This cubic integrand \( f \) is negative for \( t<[[r1]] \), positive on \( ([[r1]], [[r2]]) \), negative on \( ([[r2]], [[r3]]) \), and positive for \( t>[[r3]] \). Among the three critical numbers of \( F \), which one is a LOCAL MAXIMUM?
solution: \( F' = f \) changes from negative to positive at \( x=[[r1]] \) (local minimum), from positive to negative at \( x=[[r2]] \) (local maximum), and from negative to positive at \( x=[[r3]] \) (local minimum). So the local maximum occurs at \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is a local MINIMUM of \( F \) (the smallest critical number), not the local maximum.
This is a local MINIMUM of \( F \) (the largest critical number), not the local maximum.
This adds the two local-minimum locations instead of picking the local-maximum location.
This averages all three critical numbers rather than identifying the sign-change pattern.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This multiplies the two local-minimum locations instead of picking the local-maximum location.

stem: Let \( F(x) = \int_{[[k]]}^{x} \left( ([[a]])t^{2} + ([[b]])t + ([[c]]) \right) dt \). Find the \( x \)-coordinate of the inflection point of \( F \), i.e. the value at which \( F''(x) = 0 \).
solution: Here \( F'(x) = ([[a]])x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \), so \( F''(x) = 2([[a]])x + ([[b]]) \). This is the derivative of \( f(t)=([[a]])t^2+([[b]])t+([[c]]) \), i.e. \( F \) has an inflection point exactly where the integrand \( f \) has its own critical point (vertex). Setting \( F''(x)=0 \) gives \( x = -\frac{[[b]]}{2([[a]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of 2 from differentiating \( ([[a]])x^{2} \), using \( F'=0 \) logic instead of \( F''=0 \).
This drops the minus sign on the vertex formula.
This inverts the roles of \( a \) and \( b \) in the vertex formula.
This reports the constant term of \( f \), which plays no role in locating the inflection point.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Water flows into a tank at a rate of \( r(t) = ([[m]])t + ([[n]]) \) gallons per minute for \( t \ge 0 \) (negative \( r(t) \) means water is being drained). Let \( V(t) = \int_{0}^{t} r(s)\, ds \) be the net volume added by time \( t \). At what time \( t>0 \) is \( V(t) \) at a critical point (a local extreme value of the accumulated volume)?
solution: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, \( V'(t) = r(t) = ([[m]])t + ([[n]]) \). The accumulated volume is at a critical point when the inflow rate is zero: \( ([[m]])t + ([[n]]) = 0 \), so \( t = -\frac{[[n]]}{[[m]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the minus sign when isolating \( t \).
This drops the minus sign and additionally has the wrong sign convention.
This inverts the ratio of the coefficients.
This multiplies the coefficients instead of solving the linear equation \( r(t)=0 \) for \( t \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
